title: Definition of Digital Payment Token (DPT)
content: Under Section 2 of the Payment Services Act 2019 (PS Act), a digital payment token (DPT) is defined as any digital representation of value that is not denominated in any currency and is not pegged by its issuer to any currency, is a medium of exchange accepted by the public or a section of the public as payment for goods or services or for the discharge of a debt, can be transferred, stored, or traded electronically, and satisfies such other characteristics as the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) may prescribe. This definition deliberately excludes stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies (which may fall under the e-money category) and securities tokens (regulated under the Securities and Futures Act). The definition captures cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Ether that function primarily as media of exchange. The PS Act amended in January 2020 brought DPT services within MAS's regulatory perimeter for the first time, making Singapore one of the earliest jurisdictions to establish a comprehensive licensing framework for cryptocurrency businesses. The scope of DPT is intentionally technology-neutral and focuses on the functional characteristics of the token rather than its underlying technology.

title: Licence Classes Under the Payment Services Act
content: The Payment Services Act 2019 establishes three classes of licences for payment service providers. A Standard Payment Institution (SPI) licence is for businesses with lower transaction volumes — the SPI must not process more than SGD 3 million per month in any payment service or more than SGD 6 million per month across all payment services. A Major Payment Institution (MPI) licence is required for businesses exceeding either of these thresholds. The PS Act covers seven categories of payment services: account issuance, domestic money transfer, cross-border money transfer, merchant acquisition, e-money issuance, digital payment token (DPT) services, and money-changing services. A DPT service is defined as any service of dealing in DPTs (buying or selling DPTs in exchange for money or other DPTs) or any service of facilitating the exchange of DPTs (operating a DPT exchange). The 2021 amendments to the PS Act expanded DPT services to include the service of facilitating the transmission of DPTs (custodial transfer services). Businesses providing DPT services must hold either an SPI or MPI licence depending on their transaction volumes. A separate Money-Changing Licence is available for businesses providing only money-changing services.

title: Capital Requirements for DPT Service Providers
content: MAS imposes minimum base capital requirements on licensees under the Payment Services Act. For Standard Payment Institutions providing DPT services, the minimum base capital is SGD 100,000. For Major Payment Institutions providing DPT services, the minimum base capital is SGD 250,000. The base capital must be maintained at all times in the form of paid-up ordinary share capital, retained earnings, or such other form as MAS may approve. In addition to base capital, MPI licensees are subject to ongoing financial requirements including maintaining a security deposit with MAS or obtaining a guarantee from a prescribed financial institution. The security deposit amount is calculated based on the average daily outstanding e-money float or the volume of customer money handled, as applicable. For DPT service providers specifically, MAS may impose additional capital requirements based on the risk profile of the business, including the types of DPTs dealt in, the customer base, and the volume and value of transactions. These capital requirements are significantly lower than those imposed on banks or capital markets intermediaries, reflecting the PS Act's risk-proportionate approach to regulation of payment services including DPT activities.

title: Application Process for DPT Service Licence
content: Section 13 of the PS Act sets out the application process for a payment service provider licence. Applicants must submit to MAS prescribed forms, the application fee (SGD 1,000 for SPI, SGD 1,500 for MPI), a detailed business plan describing the payment services to be provided, the technology and systems to be used, risk management frameworks, the corporate governance structure, particulars of directors and substantial shareholders including their fit and proper assessments, audited financial statements, and the applicant's AML/CFT policies and procedures. MAS will assess the application based on criteria including whether the applicant is a company incorporated in Singapore or a foreign company registered under the Companies Act, whether the directors and substantial shareholders are fit and proper, whether the applicant has adequate financial resources, whether the applicant's business plan is viable and sustainable, and whether the applicant has the operational capability and technology to provide the proposed payment services safely. MAS aims to process applications within six months but complex applications may take longer. During the transitional period following the PS Act's commencement in January 2020, existing businesses were required to apply for licences and could continue operating under transitional exemptions while their applications were being processed.

title: AML/CFT Requirements for DPT Service Providers
content: MAS Notice PSN02 on Prevention of Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism sets out comprehensive AML/CFT requirements for DPT service providers licensed under the Payment Services Act. DPT service providers must perform customer due diligence (CDD) before establishing a business relationship, before carrying out any transaction above SGD 5,000 for occasional transactions, when there is suspicion of money laundering or terrorism financing, and when there are doubts about previously obtained identification data. CDD measures include identifying and verifying the customer's identity using reliable and independent sources, identifying beneficial owners of legal entity customers (anyone with 25% or more ownership or control), understanding the nature and purpose of the business relationship, and conducting ongoing monitoring. Enhanced due diligence (EDD) is required for higher-risk customers including politically exposed persons (PEPs), customers from higher-risk jurisdictions identified by FATF, correspondent relationships, and complex or unusually large transactions. DPT service providers must file suspicious transaction reports (STRs) with the Suspicious Transaction Reporting Office (STRO) when there is suspicion of money laundering or terrorism financing, and must not tip off the customer. Records must be maintained for at least five years after the business relationship has ended.

title: Singapore Travel Rule for DPT Transfers
content: MAS Notice PSN02 implements the FATF Travel Rule for digital payment token transfers in Singapore. Under Paragraphs 13A through 13C of PSN02, DPT service providers must collect, verify, and transmit originator and beneficiary information for DPT transfers. For transfers of SGD 1,500 or more, the ordering DPT service provider must obtain and transmit to the beneficiary DPT service provider the originator's name, account number or unique transaction reference, and either the originator's address, national identity number, customer identification number, or date and place of birth. For the beneficiary, the ordering institution must obtain and transmit the beneficiary's name and account number or unique transaction reference. The beneficiary DPT service provider must verify the beneficiary's identity before making the DPT available. For transfers below SGD 1,500, the ordering institution must still obtain the originator and beneficiary names and account numbers, but is not required to verify this information unless there is a suspicion of money laundering or terrorism financing. DPT service providers must implement risk-based procedures for handling transfers that lack required originator or beneficiary information, including determining whether to execute, reject, or suspend the transfer. These requirements align with FATF Recommendation 16 and were phased in from January 2020.

title: Expanded Scope of DPT Services — 2021 Amendments
content: The Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021, which took effect in stages from January 2022 through April 2022, significantly expanded the scope of DPT services regulated under the PS Act. The amendments added the following activities to the definition of DPT services: facilitation of the transmission of DPTs, which covers custody and transfer of DPTs on behalf of customers without the provider necessarily buying or selling the tokens; and the broadened dealing definition to cover dealing in DPTs where the service provider does not take possession of the tokens but facilitates transactions as an intermediary. The 2021 amendments also introduced a new regulatory framework for cross-border transfers of DPTs, bringing DPT transmission services under the same regulatory perimeter as cross-border money transfer services. Additionally, the amendments gave MAS powers to impose requirements on DPT service providers regarding technology risk management, cybersecurity, and business continuity planning. These amendments were driven by MAS's recognition that the initial 2019 scope did not capture all material risks in the DPT ecosystem, particularly custodial services which are critical to consumer protection, and by the FATF's evolving standards on virtual asset service provider regulation.

article: MAS Guidelines on Provision of DPT Services to Retail Customers (PS-G02)
title: Retail Customer Access Restrictions for DPT Services
content: In January 2022, MAS issued guidelines (PS-G02) imposing significant restrictions on how DPT service providers interact with retail customers in Singapore. MAS stated clearly that DPT trading is highly risky and not suitable for the general public, and imposed the following requirements effective from January 2022 with full compliance required by mid-2022. DPT service providers must not market or advertise DPT services to the general public in Singapore, including through public transport, public websites, social media, broadcast media, automated teller machines, or third-party engagement. DPT service providers must not engage third parties (including social media influencers) to promote DPT services in Singapore. DPT service providers must not offer incentives to the public to trade in DPTs, such as sign-up bonuses, referral bonuses, or promotional airdrops. DPT service providers may provide factual information about their services on their own corporate websites, mobile applications, and official social media accounts, but this information must not trivialize the risks of trading DPTs. Customer suitability assessments are required before onboarding retail customers, and customers must be warned that they could lose the full amount of their DPT holdings. These restrictions represent one of the strictest retail investor protection regimes for crypto globally.

title: Capital Markets Products Test for Digital Tokens
content: Under the Securities and Futures Act (SFA), digital tokens may be classified as capital markets products if they constitute securities, units in a collective investment scheme, or derivatives contracts. MAS published A Guide to Digital Token Offerings in November 2017 (updated May 2020) clarifying how existing securities laws apply to digital tokens. A digital token constitutes a security (specifically a debenture or share) if it confers rights to participate in profits or income, confers an ownership interest in the issuer, or represents indebtedness including bonds and notes. A digital token constitutes a unit in a collective investment scheme (CIS) if investors contribute funds to a pooled portfolio managed by a third party with the intention of sharing returns. If a digital token is determined to be a capital markets product, the issuer must comply with the SFA's prospectus requirements under Part XIII (or qualify for an exemption), and intermediaries facilitating trading must hold a Capital Markets Services (CMS) licence under Section 82. CMS licence holders are subject to higher capital requirements, conduct-of-business rules, and ongoing reporting obligations compared to PS Act licensees. The determination of whether a digital token is a capital markets product under the SFA or a DPT under the PS Act is critical as it determines the applicable regulatory regime.

title: Financial Advisers Act Applicability to Digital Tokens
content: The Financial Advisers Act (FAA) applies to persons providing financial advisory services in respect of investment products, which include securities, units in collective investment schemes, life insurance policies, and such other products as MAS may prescribe. Where a digital token is classified as a capital markets product under the SFA (i.e., it constitutes a security, CIS unit, or derivative), persons providing advice on the acquisition or disposal of such tokens must hold a Financial Adviser's licence under the FAA or be an exempt financial adviser. Financial advisory services include advising others concerning any investment product, issuing or promulgating analyses or reports concerning any investment product, and marketing any collective investment scheme. Licensed financial advisers must comply with conduct-of-business requirements including performing suitability assessments (know-your-client obligations), providing balanced and fair recommendations, disclosing material interests and conflicts, and maintaining competency standards for representatives. The FAA does not apply to digital payment tokens regulated under the PS Act, as DPTs are not classified as investment products. However, where a platform offers both DPTs and security tokens, the operator may need licences under both the PS Act and the SFA/FAA, creating dual regulatory obligations.

title: Technology and Cyber Risk Management for DPT Providers
content: MAS imposes robust technology risk management requirements on DPT service providers through MAS Notice PSN01 and the broader Technology Risk Management (TRM) Guidelines. DPT service providers must establish and implement a sound and robust technology risk governance and oversight framework. This includes establishing a technology risk management framework that is approved by the board of directors or senior management, appointing an officer responsible for the management of technology risk, conducting regular risk assessments of IT systems and infrastructure, implementing security measures including access controls, encryption for data at rest and in transit, and multi-factor authentication for customer-facing systems, maintaining a comprehensive cybersecurity strategy including intrusion detection, vulnerability assessment, and penetration testing, establishing incident response and business continuity plans with regular testing, and conducting due diligence on third-party technology service providers. The MAS TRM Guidelines (January 2021 revision) specifically address cloud computing, API security, and emerging technology risks relevant to DPT service providers. DPT service providers must notify MAS of any technology incident that has a significant impact on operations or affects customer data within one hour of discovery. Failure to maintain adequate technology risk management is grounds for licence revocation.

title: Safeguarding of Customer Assets by DPT Providers
content: The 2021 amendments to the PS Act introduced Part 4A, which imposes requirements on DPT service providers to safeguard customer money and DPTs. DPT service providers must segregate customer assets from the provider's own assets and must not use customer DPTs for the provider's own purposes without the customer's prior written consent. Customer money received must be safeguarded either by depositing it in a trust account with a safeguarding institution (a bank licensed in Singapore), obtaining a guarantee or undertaking from a safeguarding institution for the amount of customer money, or depositing the money in a trust account with a designated custodian. These safeguarding requirements must be implemented within the next business day of receiving customer money. The safeguarding obligations ensure that customer assets are protected in the event of the DPT service provider's insolvency and are available for return to customers. MAS may specify additional safeguarding requirements through subsidiary legislation, including requirements for the segregation and custody of customer DPTs held on distributed ledgers. These provisions were introduced in response to high-profile failures in the crypto industry that resulted in loss of customer assets, and reflect MAS's priority of consumer protection.

title: MAS Enforcement Against Three Arrows Capital
content: In June 2022, Three Arrows Capital (3AC), a Singapore-based crypto hedge fund, collapsed owing creditors over USD 3.5 billion after making highly leveraged bets on crypto markets including a significant exposure to the failed Terra/Luna ecosystem. 3AC was registered with MAS as a fund management company under the Securities and Futures Act. In September 2022, MAS issued reprimands against 3AC for providing false information to MAS and exceeding the assets under management (AUM) threshold allowed under its registration. 3AC had been registered as a registered fund management company with an AUM limit of SGD 250 million, but had managed assets far in excess of this threshold without notifying MAS or upgrading to a licensed fund management company. Additionally, 3AC provided misleading information to MAS regarding its AUM and the identity of its directors. MAS referred the matter to the Commercial Affairs Department for potential criminal investigation. The 3AC collapse highlighted gaps in MAS's supervisory framework for crypto fund managers and contributed to MAS's decision to strengthen enforcement and impose additional restrictions on retail access to DPT services. Co-founders Zhu Su and Kyle Davies faced arrest warrants and proceedings in multiple jurisdictions.

title: MAS Response to Terraform Labs and Terra/Luna Collapse
content: The collapse of Terraform Labs' Terra/Luna ecosystem in May 2022, which wiped out approximately USD 40 billion in market value, had significant implications for Singapore's crypto regulatory landscape. Terraform Labs was incorporated in Singapore and its founder Do Kwon was based in Singapore before relocating. While Terraform Labs was not directly licensed by MAS (as its algorithmic stablecoin TerraUSD and Luna token were not classified as regulated products under the PS Act or SFA at the time), MAS had been in communication with Terraform Labs regarding the classification of its products. Following the collapse, MAS stated that it had assessed Terra/Luna and determined they did not fall within MAS's regulatory remit as Terra was an algorithmic stablecoin not backed by reserves and Luna was a governance token. However, the incident prompted MAS to accelerate its regulatory response including proposing a new framework for stablecoin regulation in Singapore (October 2022 consultation paper) and strengthening consumer protection measures. The Singapore Police Force opened investigations into Terraform Labs for potential fraud. The collapse served as a catalyst for MAS's January 2022 guidelines restricting retail crypto marketing and for the 2023 stablecoin regulatory framework requiring single-currency stablecoins pegged to the Singapore dollar or G10 currencies to be regulated.

title: MAS Framework for Single-Currency Stablecoin Regulation
content: In August 2023, MAS finalised its regulatory framework for single-currency stablecoins (SCS) pegged to the Singapore dollar or any G10 currency, issued in Singapore. The framework establishes a new category of regulated stablecoin that, once authorised, may carry the label 'MAS-regulated stablecoin'. Key requirements include: the issuer must be a company incorporated in Singapore and hold an MPI licence under the PS Act; reserve assets must be at least equivalent to 100% of the par value of outstanding stablecoins at all times and must be denominated in the same currency as the stablecoin's peg currency; reserve assets are limited to cash, cash equivalents, and debt securities with a residual maturity of no more than three months issued by the government or central bank of the peg currency country; the issuer must have the operational capability to redeem stablecoins at par value within five business days of a redemption request; the issuer must conduct independent audits of reserve assets on a monthly basis and disclose the results publicly; and the stablecoin must be issued on a permissionless public blockchain. Stablecoins that reference multiple currencies, baskets of assets, or use algorithmic stabilisation mechanisms are not eligible under this framework and remain subject to the general DPT regulatory regime.

title: MAS Powers to Revoke or Suspend Licences
content: Section 57 of the Payment Services Act grants MAS broad powers to revoke or suspend a payment service provider's licence. MAS may revoke a licence if the licensee has ceased to carry on the payment service for which it was licensed, has not commenced the service within six months of being granted the licence, has obtained the licence by fraud or misrepresentation, has contravened any provision of the PS Act or any condition of the licence, or has been convicted of an offence under the PS Act or any other written law. MAS may also revoke a licence if the licensee is being wound up, has been declared bankrupt, or is unable to meet its obligations. Before revoking a licence, MAS must give the licensee an opportunity to be heard unless the revocation is in the public interest. In addition to revocation, MAS may impose a range of supervisory actions including issuing directions under Section 44 requiring the licensee to take specific actions, imposing conditions on the licence, requiring the appointment of an approved auditor, and prohibiting the licensee from accepting new customers. MAS has used these powers to revoke licences of DPT service providers that failed to meet AML/CFT requirements or technology risk management standards.

title: Ongoing Conduct Requirements for DPT Service Providers
content: Section 37 of the PS Act and related MAS notices impose ongoing conduct-of-business requirements on licensed DPT service providers. Licensees must maintain at all times the minimum base capital and financial requirements applicable to their licence class. They must notify MAS promptly of any material change in circumstances including changes to directors, CEO, or substantial shareholders, changes in the nature or scope of payment services provided, any breach of licence conditions, any legal proceedings commenced against the licensee, and any event that may affect the licensee's ability to meet its obligations. Licensees must submit to MAS annual audited financial statements within five months of the financial year-end, half-yearly unaudited financial statements, and such other periodic returns as MAS may require. Licensees must maintain proper books and records and make them available for inspection by MAS. The licensee's auditor may be required to report to MAS on matters that, in the auditor's opinion, adversely affect the financial position of the licensee. MAS conducts periodic on-site inspections of DPT service providers to assess compliance with regulatory requirements. Non-compliance with ongoing requirements constitutes grounds for imposition of conditions on the licence, directions, or licence revocation.

article: MAS Notice PSN08 on Disclosure and Communication
title: Disclosure Requirements for DPT Service Providers
content: MAS Notice PSN08 establishes disclosure and communication requirements for DPT service providers. Before establishing a business relationship with a customer, DPT service providers must disclose in writing the risks of dealing in DPTs including the risk of total loss of value, the lack of regulatory protection for DPT transactions (as opposed to deposits or securities), and the risk that DPTs may be illiquid. Service providers must also disclose their fee structure, the process and timeline for dispute resolution and complaints handling, whether customer DPTs are commingled or segregated, the security measures in place to protect customer assets, and the service provider's policies regarding system outages and service disruptions. All disclosures must be in clear, simple language that is easily understood by a retail customer. DPT service providers must prominently display risk warnings on their websites, mobile applications, and at the point of each DPT transaction. MAS has emphasised that DPT service providers must not trivialise the risks of DPT trading or present DPT trading as a way to earn guaranteed returns. These disclosure requirements complement the marketing restrictions under PS-G02 and together form a comprehensive consumer protection framework for DPT services.

title: Criminal Offences Under the Payment Services Act
content: The Payment Services Act establishes several criminal offences with significant penalties. Operating a DPT service without a licence or exemption is an offence under Section 5, punishable by a fine of up to SGD 125,000, imprisonment for up to three years, or both. Providing false or misleading information to MAS in connection with an application for a licence or in any report required under the PS Act is an offence under Section 72, punishable by a fine of up to SGD 125,000, imprisonment for up to three years, or both. Contravening a direction issued by MAS under Section 44 is an offence punishable by a fine of up to SGD 100,000, imprisonment for up to two years, or both. For continuing offences, a further fine of up to SGD 10,000 per day may be imposed. Officers of a body corporate that commits an offence under the PS Act may be personally liable if the offence was committed with their consent, connivance, or due to their neglect. MAS also has powers to impose civil penalties and composition amounts for regulatory breaches that do not warrant criminal prosecution. The availability of both criminal and civil enforcement tools gives MAS a flexible enforcement toolkit proportionate to the severity of the contravention.

article: MAS Consultation Paper on DPT Regulation (Oct 2022)
title: Proposed Measures for DPT Consumer Protection
content: In October 2022, MAS published a consultation paper proposing additional consumer protection measures for DPT services, driven by the series of crypto failures in 2022 including Terraform Labs, Three Arrows Capital, and FTX. Key proposals included mandatory segregation of customer assets from the DPT service provider's proprietary assets, with customer assets to be held in statutory trust; prohibition on DPT service providers lending, pledging, or otherwise using customer assets without express customer consent for each specific use; mandatory risk disclosure with customers being required to pass a risk awareness assessment before being permitted to trade DPTs; restrictions on the use of leverage by retail customers for DPT trading, with MAS proposing a prohibition on lending or margin trading facilities for retail customers; requirements for DPT service providers to maintain adequate insurance or reserves to cover potential customer losses from operational failures or cyber incidents; and enhanced business conduct requirements including fair dealing, proper governance of token listings, and transparent pricing. Many of these measures were subsequently implemented through amendments to MAS Notices and Guidelines during 2023 and 2024.

article: MAS Notice PSN05 on Transitional Exemptions
title: Transitional Licensing Arrangements for DPT Providers
content: When the Payment Services Act commenced on 28 January 2020, MAS established a transitional framework to allow existing crypto businesses to continue operating while their licence applications were being processed. Under MAS Notice PSN05, businesses that were providing DPT services before the PS Act's commencement date and that submitted a licence application within the prescribed notification period were granted a transitional exemption from the licensing requirement. During the transitional period, these businesses could continue to provide DPT services subject to compliance with AML/CFT requirements under MAS Notice PSN02. MAS received over 170 licence applications for DPT services during the notification period. However, MAS applied stringent assessment criteria, and by September 2023, only approximately 19 entities had been granted full DPT service provider licences, while a significant number of applicants had withdrawn or had their applications rejected. Common grounds for rejection included inadequate AML/CFT controls, insufficient technology risk management frameworks, lack of operational readiness, and concerns about the fitness and propriety of key personnel. MAS's rigorous approach to licensing reflected its stated policy of being welcoming of innovation but highly attentive to risks, particularly AML/CFT and consumer protection risks.

title: Cross-Border Regulatory Cooperation for DPT Services
content: Section 103 of the Payment Services Act empowers MAS to cooperate with foreign regulatory authorities in the supervision and enforcement of DPT service providers operating across borders. MAS may share information with foreign counterparts where such sharing is necessary for the performance of MAS's functions or the foreign authority's functions, subject to confidentiality safeguards. Singapore has established mutual recognition arrangements and regulatory cooperation agreements with multiple jurisdictions regarding the oversight of DPT services. MAS participates actively in international standard-setting bodies including the Financial Stability Board (FSB), the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), and the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO) in developing global standards for crypto-asset regulation. Singapore's membership in FATF means that MAS must ensure its DPT regulatory framework meets FATF standards, including the implementation of the Travel Rule. MAS has also participated in cross-border supervisory colleges for significant DPT service providers that operate across multiple jurisdictions. This international cooperation framework is critical given the inherently cross-border nature of crypto-asset activities and the risks of regulatory arbitrage if jurisdictions apply inconsistent standards.

article: MAS Notice PSN10 on Fit and Proper Criteria
title: Fit and Proper Requirements for DPT Service Provider Personnel
content: MAS imposes fit and proper requirements on key individuals associated with DPT service providers including directors, chief executive officers, substantial shareholders, and partners. Under MAS Guidelines on Fit and Proper Criteria and the PS Act, these individuals must demonstrate honesty, integrity, and reputation — the individual must not have been convicted of any offence involving fraud, dishonesty, or moral turpitude, must not be an undischarged bankrupt, and must not have been the subject of any civil or criminal proceedings involving fraud. They must demonstrate competence and capability — possessing the relevant qualifications, training, skills, and experience appropriate to the nature and scale of the DPT service provider's business. They must also demonstrate financial soundness — the individual must not be subject to any unsatisfied judgement or award and must be able to fulfil financial obligations as they fall due. MAS may require the removal of any director, CEO, or officer of a DPT service provider who fails to meet fit and proper criteria. The assessment is conducted at the time of licence application and on an ongoing basis, with the licensee required to notify MAS of any changes in the fitness and propriety of key individuals within 14 days of becoming aware of such changes.

title: MAS Powers for User Protection Measures
content: Section 80A of the PS Act, introduced by the 2021 amendments, grants MAS specific powers to impose user protection measures on DPT service providers. MAS may issue directions to a licensee to take any action that MAS considers necessary for the protection of the interests of customers or prospective customers of the licensee. These directions may include requiring the licensee to cease providing specific DPT services, restricting the types of customers to whom DPT services may be provided, imposing limits on transaction values or volumes, requiring enhanced disclosures to customers, and mandating specific safeguarding arrangements for customer DPTs. MAS may also impose requirements relating to the manner in which a licensee holds or deals with customer DPTs, including requirements for the segregation, custody, and insurance of customer assets. These powers are in addition to MAS's general power to issue directions under Section 44 of the PS Act. The breadth of these user protection powers reflects MAS's post-2022 emphasis on consumer protection in the DPT space and provides MAS with the flexibility to respond quickly to emerging risks without the need for legislative amendments. MAS has stated that it will use these powers proactively where it identifies practices that may put customer assets at risk.

title: MAS Enforcement — Licence Revocations and Withdrawals
content: MAS has taken enforcement action against multiple DPT service provider applicants and licensees. Between 2020 and 2024, MAS rejected or saw the withdrawal of over 150 DPT licence applications out of approximately 170 received, reflecting its rigorous assessment standards. Notable enforcement actions include the revocation of Hodlnaut's exemption status in March 2023 after the platform suspended withdrawals and was placed under interim judicial management, with MAS citing concerns about the firm's misrepresentation of its exposure to the Terra/Luna ecosystem. MAS also took action against Vauld (previously Defi Payments), which suspended withdrawals in July 2022, directing the company to return customer assets. In August 2023, MAS issued prohibition orders against certain individuals involved in the management of failed DPT service providers, barring them from performing regulated activities. MAS has also issued warnings to overseas platforms that solicited Singapore customers without holding the appropriate licence or exemption. These enforcement actions have reinforced MAS's message that its DPT regulatory framework will be rigorously enforced and that consumer protection is a supervisory priority. The high rejection rate of licence applications has been both praised for maintaining standards and criticised for creating barriers to entry.
